title: Vesicoureteral Reflux: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Vesicoureteral Reflux
Identify key diagnostic features of Vesicoureteral Reflux
Apply clinical reasoning to Vesicoureteral Reflux management
Recognize common pitfalls in Vesicoureteral Reflux

sections.title: Overview of Vesicoureteral Reflux
sections.content:
**Vesicoureteral Reflux**



**Key Points:**
• Retrograde flow of urine from bladder into ureter/renal pelvis
• Most common urological anomaly in children; presents with recurrent UTIs
• Grading I-V based on VCUG: I (ureter only) to V (gross dilation + tortuous ureter + loss of papillary impressions)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Retrograde flow of urine from bladder into ureter/renal pelvis
• Most common urological anomaly in children; presents with recurrent UTIs

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Grading I-V based on VCUG: I (ureter only) to V (gross dilation + tortuous ureter + loss of papillary impressions)
• Grades I-III: conservative management (antibiotic prophylaxis, most resolve spontaneously)
• Grades IV-V or breakthrough infections: surgical reimplantation (ureteral reimplant) or endoscopic injection (Deflux)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Vesicoureteral Reflux:**

• Grading I-V based on VCUG: I (ureter only) to V (gross dilation + tortuous ureter + loss of papillary impressions)
• Grades I-III: conservative management (antibiotic prophylaxis, most resolve spontaneously)
• Grades IV-V or breakthrough infections: surgical reimplantation (ureteral reimplant) or endoscopic injection (Deflux)
